source_title: December 2025 Accreditation Actions and Public Disclosure Statements - SACSCOC - Public Disclosure Statement
notes: Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges (SACSCOC) http://www.sacscoc.org Disclosure Statement Regarding the Status of UNIVERSITY OF LYNCHBURG Lynchburg, Virginia Issued January 8, 2026, by SACSCOC The following publicly available information is provided by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on Colleges (SACSCOC) concerning the accreditation of the University of Lynchburg. The information presented below is in accordance with the SACSCOC disclosure policy; SACSCOC staff cannot comment further on questions specifically related to the University of Lynchburg. The institution reviewed this statement prior to public posting and has 60 days to provide an official comment to be added to this disclosure regarding the action taken by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees. On December 7, 2025, the SACSCOC Board of Trustees denied reaffirmation, continued accreditation, and continued the University of Lynchburg on Warning for twelve months. The institution’s next review will occur in December 2026. What is the accreditation status of the University of Lynchburg? The University of Lynchburg remains an accredited member of SACSCOC while denied reaffirmation and on the sanction of Warning. Because SACSCOC is an institutional accreditor, this status applies to the all components of the institution—all programs, branch campuses, off-campus sites, and distance education programs as reported to SACSCOC. What does it mean to be continued in accreditation and continued on Warning? The sanction of Warning is imposed by the SACSCOC Board of Trustees in the early stages of institutional following a determination of non-compliance with one or more of the Core Requirements and/or Standards of the Principles of Accreditation, the accreditation standards of SACSCOC; failure to make timely and significant progress toward correcting the deficiencies that led to the findings of non-compliance; or failure to comply with SACSCOC policies and procedures. In December 2026, the University of Lynchburg will have been on Warning for two (2) years, which is the maximum total time allowed during one monitoring period. An institution’s accreditation can be extended into a second monitoring period for Good Cause. For additional information about sanctions, see the SACSCOC policy entitled Sanctions, Denial of Reaffirmation, and Removal from Membership. Why was the University of Lynchburg denied reaffirmation and continued on Warning? The University of Lynchburg was denied reaffirmation and continued on Warning because the SACSCOC Board of Trustees determined that the institution failed to demonstrate compliance with Standard 8.2.a (Student outcomes: educational outcomes) and Standard 13.3 (Financial responsibility) of the Principles of Accreditation. These standards expect the institution to identify expected outcomes, assess the extent to which it achieves these outcomes, and provide evidence of seeking improvement based on analysis of the results for each of its educational programs; and to manage its financial resources in a responsible manner. A Special Committee visit was not authorized. This determination follows a previous action taken by the Board in December 2024 which also indicated non-compliance with these standards, hence the continuation of the denial of reaffirmation and sanction of Warning. What will happen in December 2026? The SACSCOC Board of Trustees will consider the accreditation status of the University of Lynchburg following a review of a Second Monitoring Report submitted by the institution addressing the standards cited above. The maximum total time during a monitoring period that an institution may be on sanction is two (2) years. In December 2026, Blue Mountain Christian University will have been on Warning for one (1) year. For additional information about sanctions, see the SACSCOC policy entitled Sanctions, Denial of Reaffirmation, and Removal from Membership. Why was Blue Mountain Christian University denied reaffirmation and placed on Warning? Blue Mountain Christian University was denied reaffirmation and placed on Warning because the SACSCOC Board of Trustees determined that the institution has not yet demonstrated compliance with the Principles of Accreditation, namely, Core Requirement 13.1 (Financial resources) and Standard 13.3 (Financial responsibility). Core Requirement 13.1 expects an institution to have sound financial resources and a demonstrated, stable financial base to support the mission of the institution and the scope of its programs and services. Standard 13.3 expects an institution to manage its financial resources in a responsible manner. A Special Committee visit was not authorized. The SACSCOC Board of Trustees will consider the accreditation status of Blue Mountain Christian University following a review of a Monitoring Report submitted by the institution addressing the standards cited above. notes: March 26, 2026 Dr. Joseph Pizzorno Interim President Bastyr University 14500 Juanita Drive NE Kenmore, WA 98028 Dear President Pizzorno: This letter serves as formal notification and official record of action taken by the Northwest Commission on Colleges and Universities (NWCCU) at its meeting on January 27-30, 2026, concerning the Fall 2025 Special Report of Bastyr University. Accreditation Reaffirm Accreditation: Remove the Sanction of Show Cause, Accept the Report Status of Previous Recommendations Addressed in this Evaluation • Recommendation 1: Fall 2024 Financial Resources Review - Fulfilled • Recommendation 2: Fall 2024 Financial Resources Review - Continued as Needs Improvement and revised to Recommendation 1: Fall 2025 Special Report Recommendations Substantially in Compliance but in Need of Improvement The Commission recommends that Bastyr University: Recommendation 1: Fall 2025 Special Report - Engage in effective financial planning that results in appropriate risk management strategies, such as the building of financial reserves, to ensure short-term and long-term financial health and stability. (2020 Standard(s) 2.E.1; 2.E.2) Future Evaluations • Year 6 - Standard 2 - Policies, Regulations, and Financial Review Spring 2026 • Financial Resources Review Spring 2027 o Recommendation 1: Fall 2025 Special Report • Year 7 - Evaluation of Institutional Effectiveness Spring 2027 o Recommendation 1: Fall 2024 Ad Hoc Report with Visit o Recommendation 3: Fall 2019 Mission Fulfillment and Sustainability NWCCU is committed to an accreditation process that adds value to institutions while contributing to public accountability, and we thank you for your continued support of this process. If you have questions about any of the information in this letter, please contact your staff liaison, Dr. Teresa Rivenes, at trivenes@nwccu.org. Sincerely, Selena M. notes: February 9, 2026 Dr. Nick Swayne President North Idaho College 1000 West Garden Avenue Coeur d'Alene, ID 83814 Dear President Swayne: This letter serves as formal notification and official record of action taken by the Northwest Commission on Colleges and Universities (NWCCU) at its meeting on January 27-30, 2026, concerning the Fall 2025 Mid-Cycle Review of North Idaho College. Accreditation Reaffirm Accreditation: Remove the Sanction of Probation, Accept the Report Status of Previous Recommendations Addressed in this Evaluation • Recommendation 1: Spring 2020 Mission Fulfillment and Sustainability - Continued as Needs Improvement • Recommendation 2: Spring 2020 Mission Fulfillment and Sustainability - Continued as Needs Improvement • Recommendation 2: Spring 2022 Special Report - Fulfilled • Recommendation 3: Spring 2022 Special Report - Fulfilled • Recommendation 7: Spring 2023 Special Report – Fulfilled • Recommendation 1: Fall 2024 Special Report - Fulfilled Recommendations Substantially in Compliance but in Need of Improvement The Commission recommends that North Idaho College: • Recommendation 1: Spring 2020 Mission Fulfillment and Sustainability - Establish meaningful indicators (metrics) to align with and measure institutional goals and objectives in its evaluation, planning, and resource allocation processes to support institutional effectiveness and student achievement. If you have questions about any of the information in this letter, please contact your staff liaison, Dr. Ed Harri, at eharri@nwccu.org. Sincerely, Selena M. action_label_raw: Accept the Report, Issue a Sanction of Probation on Recommendation 1 of the Fall 2025 Financial Resources Review
source_title: The Seattle School of Theology and Psychology - NWCCU - Institution Notification Letter
notes: February 6, 2026 Dr. J. McNeil President The Seattle School of Theology and Psychology 1130 Rainier Ave South Seattle, WA 98144 Dear President McNeil: This letter serves as formal notification and official record of action taken by the Northwest Commission on Colleges and Universities (NWCCU) at its meeting on January 27-30, 2026, concerning the Fall 2025 Financial Resources Review of The Seattle School of Theology and Psychology. Accreditation Accept the Report, Issue a Sanction of Probation on Recommendation 1 of the Fall 2025 Financial Resources Review Recommendations Out of Compliance The Commission finds that the following Recommendations are areas where The Seattle School of Theology and Psychology is out of compliance with the NWCCU Standards for Accreditation. According to U.S. Department of Education Regulation 34 CFR 602.20 and Commission Policy, the Commission requires that The Seattle School of Theology and Psychology take appropriate action to ensure these Recommendations are addressed and resolved within the prescribed four-year period, no later than February 6, 2030. The Commission recommends that The Seattle School of Theology and Psychology: • Recommendation 1: Fall 2025 Financial Resources Review - Clearly demonstrate financial stability, including sufficient cash flow, to achieve and fulfill the institution’s mission. (2020 Standard(s) 2.E.1; ER 19) Probation Because of the significant financial issues at the institution, the Commission has issued the sanction of Probation, as stated in NWCCU’s Accreditation Actions Policy: NWCCU may place an institution on Probation and notes its Accreditation is in jeopardy when it does not appear to be in compliance with Standards for Accreditation, Eligibility Requirements, Policies, or applicable federal regulations, and there is evidence non- compliance is sufficiently serious, extensive, or substantial, that it raises concern about: quality of student learning experience; capacity to make appropriate improvements within a short period of time; capacity to sustain itself in the long term such that a teach-out plan is required; or information collected through monitoring activities suggests there are serious concerns related to student achievement, viability and capacity, or financial health. As a requirement of the Probation sanction, the institution will submit a Financial Resources Review (FRR) in Spring 2026 demonstrating the efforts the institution has made to come into compliance with identified NWCCU Standards for Accreditation (34 CFR 602.20(a)(2)). The institution must also develop teach-out plans, following the Procedures to Close a Program, Additional Location, Branch Campus, or Institution Policy. As noted in the Policy, “per Federal Regulation 34 CFR 602.24(c)(3), a teach-out plan must include (1) a list of currently enrolled students, (2) a list of academic 8060 165th Ave NE, Suite 200 | Redmond, WA 98052 | nwccu.org programs offered by the institution, and (3) the names of other institutions that offer similar programs and that could potentially enter into a teach-out agreement with the institution.” The teach out-plan, including these requirements, must be submitted to NWCCU within 60 days from receipt of this letter. In compliance with NWCCU’s Public Disclosures by Institutions Policy and per Federal Regulation 34 CFR 602.26(b)(e), the institution must notify current and prospective students and display an indicator of the sanction and non-compliance on its website within seven (7) calendar days of receipt of this letter. Future Evaluations • Financial Resources Review Spring 2026 o Recommendation 1: Fall 2025 Financial Resources Review • Year 6 - Standard 2 - Policies, Regulations, and Financial Review Fall 2026 • Year 7 - Evaluation of Institutional Effectiveness Fall 2027 o Recommendation 1: Fall 2023 Mid-Cycle Review o Recommendation 2: Fall 2023 Mid-Cycle Review NWCCU is committed to an accreditation process that adds value to institutions while contributing to public accountability, and we thank you for your continued support of this process. If you have questions about any of the information in this letter, please contact your staff liaison, Dr. Teresa Rivenes, at trivenes@nwccu.org. Sincerely, Selena M. Grace President cc: Ms. Katie Clum, Instructional Designer Mr. Dana Scannell, Board Chair Mr. Michael Meotti, Executive Director, Washington Student Achievement Council 8060 165th Ave NE, Suite 200 | Redmond, WA 98052 | nwccu.org

Issue Sanction of Warning on Recommendations 1 and 2 Spring 2025 Special Report. Recommendations Out of Compliance The Commission finds that the following Recommendations are areas where Wenatchee Valley College is out of compliance with the NWCCU Standards for Accreditation. According to U.S. Department of Education Regulation 34 CFR 602.20 and Commission Policy, the Commission requires that Wenatchee Valley College take appropriate action to ensure these Recommendations are addressed and resolved within the prescribed four-year period, by no later than August 2029. The Commission recommends that Wenatchee Valley College: • Recommendation 1: Spring 2025 Special Report - All administrators and supervisors receive ongoing quality training on ethical management, ethical operations, transparent communication, and the respectful, consistent, fair, timely, and ethical handling of complaints and grievances. (2020 Standard(s) 2.D.2; ER 7; ER 8) • Recommendation 2: Spring 2025 Special Report - Employees receive ongoing quality training on effective workplace communication, preventing bullying, harassment, and discrimination, and how the complaint and grievance process works (including confidentiality).
